name: dev.ucp.common.identity_linking
title: Identity Linking
description: Capability schema for identity linking. Businesses declare the user-authenticated scopes they offer in a flat 'scopes' map. Each key is the OAuth scope string as it appears on the wire ('{capability}:{scope}', e.g. 'dev.ucp.shopping.order:read'). Scope presence implies that the corresponding operations require user authentication. Operations not gated by any listed scope operate at whatever access level the business permits; UCP does not prescribe a default.
$comment: Auth mechanism: OAuth 2.0 with RFC 8414 discovery on the business domain is the always-available baseline. 'config.providers' is additive metadata declaring trusted external identity providers from whom platforms MAY chain identity via RFC 8693 token exchange + RFC 7523 JWT bearer, skipping a fresh browser-based OAuth flow. The 'provider.type' discriminator is a REQUIRED open string: 'oauth2' is the only type defined in this version, and future versions MAY define additional non-OAuth mechanisms (e.g., wallet attestation) as non-breaking extensions. Per-type fields are enforced via 'if/then' so unrecognized types stay valid and are filtered by platforms at runtime. 'config' is open for non-breaking extension at the field level; platforms MUST ignore unrecognized sibling fields. Businesses MUST NOT list their own authorization server in 'providers' (chaining-to-self is degenerate; direct OAuth is already available via discovery). When no listed provider is supported by the platform, the platform MUST fall back to direct OAuth on the business domain via discovery; it MUST NOT abort solely because 'providers' is non-empty.

scope_policy.title: Scope Policy
scope_policy.description: Per-scope policy and metadata — auth constraints (e.g. min_acr, max_token_age), declarative metadata (e.g. claims produced, consent descriptions), or any other scope-specific configuration. An empty object means user authentication is required with no additional policy. Open for non-breaking extension.

description.description: Optional human-readable description of the scope that platforms can use to present and explain context (requirement and value) to the user.

scope_token.description: OAuth scope string formed by joining a capability name and a scope name with a colon: '{capability}:{scope}', e.g. 'dev.ucp.shopping.order:read'. Capability names use reverse-DNS naming; scope names denote the permission granted, defined by each capability's spec (e.g. 'read', 'manage', 'create'). Platforms request these strings verbatim in OAuth 'scope' parameters; issued tokens carry them in the 'scope' claim.

provider.title: Identity Provider
provider.description: A trusted identity provider for delegated authentication, keyed by the 'type' discriminator. 'oauth2' denotes an OAuth 2.0 / OIDC authorization server. Future versions MAY define additional types (e.g. wallet attestation) as non-breaking extensions; platforms MUST treat entries whose 'type' they do not support as filtered out (see Provider Selection).
provider.$comment: Discriminator design: 'type' is a REQUIRED open string, and per-type constraints are applied via 'if/then' branches (not 'oneOf'). This is deliberate. A closed 'oneOf' over enumerated arms would reject any entry whose 'type' is unknown to the schema version doing the validation, which contradicts the forward-compatibility rule that platforms filter unsupported types at runtime rather than rejecting the business config. 'if/then' enforces the known type's fields and lets unrecognized types validate as bare objects, so a platform pinned to an older schema does not reject a newer mechanism. This also matches UCP's house composition pattern (allOf/conditional) rather than the structural, fixed-set 'oneOf' in fulfillment_destination.json.

type.description: Provider mechanism discriminator. 'oauth2' for OAuth 2.0 / OIDC authorization servers. Additional values MAY be defined by future versions; the value is an open string, not a closed enum, so unrecognized types remain valid and are filtered at runtime.

allOf.$comment: oauth2 providers discover their authorization server via 'auth_url'. Verification-only mechanisms (e.g. wallet attestation) discover by other means and are added as sibling conditional branches without touching this one.

auth_url.description: Base URL for authorization server metadata discovery. Platforms resolve metadata via RFC 8414 (/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server) with OIDC fallback (/.well-known/openid-configuration) on 404.

platform_schema.title: Identity Linking (Platform)
platform_schema.description: Platform-level identity linking capability declaration. Platforms advertise support for identity linking; no auth-specific config is required.

business_schema.title: Identity Linking (Business)
business_schema.description: Business-level identity linking configuration. Businesses declare the user-authenticated scopes they offer in 'config.scopes'.

providers.description: Map of trusted external identity providers keyed by reverse-domain identifier. Declares which upstream IdPs the business will accept JWT bearer assertions from for the Accelerated IdP Flow (chaining via RFC 8693 + RFC 7523). This field is additive: direct OAuth against the business domain via RFC 8414 discovery is always available regardless of 'providers' content. Businesses MUST NOT list their own authorization server here — chaining-to-self is degenerate, and direct OAuth covers that path. When absent, empty, or when no listed provider is supported by the platform, platforms run direct OAuth on the business domain.

scopes.description: Map of user-authenticated scopes offered by this business. Each key is an OAuth scope string formed as '{capability}:{scope}' (e.g. 'dev.ucp.shopping.order:read'). Scope presence in this map declares that the corresponding operations require a user identity token. Operations not gated by any listed scope operate at whatever access level the business permits; UCP does not prescribe a default. Each value is a per-scope policy object (empty object means user auth required with no additional policy).
